core.intro: Across Australia, dragon fruit (pitaya) is grown the same way everywhere: a climbing cactus trained up a sturdy post or trellis, in full sun and free-draining soil, watered steadily while it is flowering and fruiting. The commercial crop sits in Queensland, the Northern Territory, northern New South Wales and parts of Western Australia, but the plant will fruit in any warm, frost-free spot, including a large pot. The state sections cover the climate, growing areas, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Dragon fruit is the fruit of several climbing cacti. The most widely grown is Hylocereus undatus (now also written Selenicereus undatus), with pink or red skin and white flesh dotted with tiny edible black seeds. Red and magenta-fleshed types belong mostly to Hylocereus polyrhizus and its relatives, and the yellow pitaya is Selenicereus megalanthus, with yellow skin and sweet, clear-white flesh. Within each group there are dozens of named selections (nurseries often call them clones rather than true varieties) that differ in flavour, sweetness, skin and flesh colour, and how readily they set fruit on their own. Decide on the flesh colour and flavour you want first, then check the selection's pollination needs before you buy. The in-stock table above shows the named selections available right now and what they cost.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two plants?
sections.body: It depends on the selection. Many white-fleshed Hylocereus undatus plants set a crop with their own pollen, but a lot of named cultivars, especially red and pink-fleshed types, are self-incompatible and fruit only with pollen from a different variety. Even a self-fertile plant usually sets bigger, more reliable fruit when it is cross-pollinated. The large, scented, white flowers open for only a night or two and are pollinated by moths and bats rather than bees, so fruit set can be poor where those night visitors are scarce. The reliable fix is hand pollination: on the night a flower opens, or first thing the next morning, brush pollen from one plant onto the stigma of another. If you only have room for one plant, choose a white-fleshed, self-fertile selection.

sections.heading: Support, soil and planting
sections.body: Dragon fruit is a climbing cactus that clings with aerial roots, so it needs a strong support to carry a heavy mass of stems: a single stout post (concrete, or untreated hardwood) about 2 m high with a frame on top, or a trellis or fence. Train one leader up to the top, rubbing off the side shoots on the way, then let the branches cascade over, because it is the hanging branches that flower and fruit. Red-fleshed types tend to sprawl and are often grown along a fence instead. Plant on a free-draining mound, because the roots are shallow (most sit in the top 15 to 30 cm of soil) and will rot in wet or salty ground. The plant is otherwise unfussy about soil and responds well to plenty of organic matter. Container-grown plants can go in through the warm months (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Despite being a cactus, dragon fruit has a fairly high water requirement once it is flowering and fruiting, and it crops best on steady moisture rather than the boom and bust a desert cactus tolerates. Keep the soil from drying out completely while fruit is filling, because a swing from dry to wet makes the fruit split, and mulch the mound (kept off the stem) to even out moisture. At the same time it hates waterlogging, so the answer is frequent light watering on free-draining ground, not heavy soakings. Feed it more like a fruiting vine than a desert plant: a balanced NPK fertiliser plus organic matter and a little lime, with more through the growing and fruiting months and less nitrogen as it rests before flowering. The Northern Territory grower guide suggests a program of NPK with dolomite and an organic fertiliser on alternate months at about 100 g per plant, building up as the plant grows, plus a foliar feed while the fruit develops.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Dragon fruit ripens fast: the fruit is ready about a month (roughly 28 to 35 days) after the flower closes, once the skin has coloured up fully and the little scales on the skin begin to wither. It is non-climacteric, which means it does not sweeten after picking, so leave it on the plant until it is fully coloured for the best flavour and sugar. A healthy plant throws several flushes of flowers across the warm season, so the harvest comes in waves rather than all at once. Ripe fruit holds on the plant for a week or two if the birds leave it alone, and once picked it keeps for several weeks in the fridge. Average fruit weight is around 350 g.

sections.heading: Eating dragon fruit
sections.body: The simplest way to eat a dragon fruit is chilled: halve it and scoop the flesh out with a spoon, much like a kiwi fruit. The flesh is crisp and mildly sweet, with the tiny black seeds adding a gentle crunch, and white-fleshed types are milder than the deeper-flavoured reds. It is good in fruit salads and smoothies, and the flesh freezes well, whole or pulped. Because the flavour is delicate, a squeeze of lime or a little sugar lifts it.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a cutting-grown plant of a named selection rather than a seedling. Dragon fruit does not come true from seed, and seedlings are slow and unreliable to fruit, while a rooted cutting from a proven plant fruits far sooner and to type. A healthy cutting is firm and green, usually around 30 cm long; once potted it can begin fruiting within a year or two. Check the label for the flesh colour and, importantly, whether the selection needs a second variety to pollinate it, so you are not left with a plant that flowers but never sets fruit.

faqs.q: Can I grow dragon fruit in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, and it suits a courtyard or a frost-prone garden because you can move it. Use a large, heavy pot (the top growth gets tall and topples a light one), a free-draining cactus or potting mix, and set in a stake or small trellis for it to climb. Stand it in full sun, water when the mix is nearly dry, and feed through the warm months. In cool districts move it under cover for winter.

faqs.q: How soon will a dragon fruit start cropping?
faqs.a: Quickly, by fruit-tree standards. With a warm position and a sturdy support to fill, plants commonly flower in their first or second summer and build to a solid crop by the third or fourth year as the canopy grows. Cold, deep shade, or a lack of night pollinators are the usual reasons a young plant stalls. Patience over the first couple of summers, while the framework of branches develops, pays off.

faqs.q: Does dragon fruit need full sun, and can the stems sunburn?
faqs.a: It crops best in full sun, but the green stems can scald in extreme heat, especially on young plants or freshly exposed growth. In very hot, dry inland gardens, give young plants afternoon shade or light shadecloth until they are established, and avoid suddenly exposing shaded stems to fierce sun. Established plants in coastal and humid areas usually take full sun without trouble.

No. The stems are full of water, so a frost ruptures the cells and can kill or badly set back a plant, and growth stalls once nights fall below about 10 degrees. It grows best between roughly 20 and 32 degrees. In frost-prone districts grow it in a warm, sheltered spot against a sun-facing wall, or in a pot you can move under cover for winter.

QLD.intro: Growing dragon fruit in Queensland. Queensland and the Northern Territory grow most of Australia's dragon fruit, and the warm Queensland climate suits it from the tropical north to the subtropical south-east.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Dragon fruit thrives in Queensland's warm, humid summers. It is a long-day plant, so the flowers come on through the longer days of spring and summer and the fruit follows in the warm months. The main thing to manage is wet-season rain at flowering, which can drop flowers and, later, split ripening fruit, so steady moisture and sharp drainage matter more here than extra water. From the wet tropics to the subtropical south-east the plant grows readily, and only the coolest, frostier inland is marginal.

sections.heading: Where dragon fruit grows in Queensland
sections.body: Growers have planted dragon fruit on the Atherton Tablelands behind Cairns since about 2008, and it is also grown in the dry tropics around Townsville and the Burdekin, in the Bundaberg and Wide Bay district, and in Central Queensland around Rockhampton on the Capricornia coast. Specialist cutting growers as far south as Agnes Water carry scores of selections, and backyard plants crop well across the warmer parts of the state, including the Brisbane area.

sections.heading: Harvest window in Queensland
sections.body: Expect fruit in the warm half of the year. In the subtropical south-east the season runs from around December to April, while the tropical north can carry fruit over an even longer warm period. Plants flower and fruit in repeated flushes, so picking is spread across several weeks. Early growers in the Brisbane area recorded a main crop in autumn and a lighter second flush later in the year, which is typical of how the plant behaves toward its cooler southern edge.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Queensland
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native and widespread across eastern Australia with a very wide host range, so net or bag fruit and follow local controls. Birds, possums, rats and flying foxes all take ripe fruit, which is why commercial plants are netted. Queensland's humidity also brings stem and fruit rots: a bacterial soft rot can follow sunburn or injury, and a fungus can spot the stems, so keep the canopy open and airy and cut out damaged growth.

faqs.q: Which dragon fruit suits humid coastal Queensland?
faqs.a: Vigorous white-fleshed Hylocereus undatus selections are the easiest starting point, because they handle the humidity and many set fruit on their own. The richer red-fleshed kinds crop well too, but usually want a second variety and a little help with pollination. On the Tablelands and in the drier tropics almost any selection will do; near the wet coast, favour an open, free-draining, sunny site to keep rots down.

faqs.q: Is dragon fruit a weed risk?
faqs.a: It is worth being tidy. Dragon fruit is a cactus, and segments that fall or are dumped can take root, while birds spread seed, though it is not a declared weed. Do not dump prunings in bushland (bag or compost them), clean up fallen fruit, and if you garden near a reserve keep an eye out for stray seedlings.

WA.intro: Growing dragon fruit in Western Australia. The plant grows in WA's warm north and the Gascoyne, and in sheltered, frost-free spots on the warm coast, but quarantine limits which interstate nurseries can post live plants here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Dragon fruit needs heat and a frost-free position, both of which WA's tropical north and warm coast can provide. The Ord River irrigation area around Kununurra and the Gascoyne around Carnarvon have the long warm season it likes, and warm coastal districts can grow it in a sunny, sheltered spot. The hot, dry inland and the far north supply plenty of heat but need irrigation and, for young plants, some shade against the fierce summer sun.

sections.heading: Where dragon fruit grows in WA
sections.body: Carnarvon, on the Gascoyne River, is one of WA's major horticultural districts, and the state agriculture department grows dragon fruit there among its tropical fruit collection. The Ord River irrigation area at Kununurra and pockets of the Pilbara have the tropical heat for it, and gardeners in the warm coastal suburbs grow it in backyards up a sunny wall or post.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: As everywhere, the fruit follows the warm season. Flowers open through the long days of late spring and summer and the fruit ripens about a month later, so the WA crop fills from summer into autumn, earliest and longest in the hot north and a little later on the cooler coast. Keep the water up steadily as the fruit fills, because a dry-then-wet swing in the warm Gascoyne and inland conditions will split the skins.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's drier air keeps the stem and fruit rots that trouble the humid eastern states more in check, though they can still follow sunburn or injury. The main animal pests are birds and rats after the ripe fruit, so netting helps. Mediterranean fruit fly is established across much of WA and will attack soft-skinned fruit, so follow local fruit-fly controls.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants and cuttings from other states must meet WA import conditions, and many eastern nurseries will not post to WA at all. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, which is why the WA list looks shorter than the eastern states.

faqs.q: Which dragon fruit suits Perth and WA's warm coast?
faqs.a: On the warm coast a white-fleshed, self-fertile selection is the safe choice, because it will set fruit without a partner and copes with the milder coastal warmth. If you have room for two plants, adding a second variety lifts the crop. Up north in the heat almost any selection will fruit, given water and a little shade for young plants.

faqs.q: Can dragon fruit handle Perth's sandy soils and dry summer?
faqs.a: Well, in fact. Free-draining sand suits a cactus far better than heavy clay, as long as you add compost for the shallow roots to feed in, mulch to hold moisture, and water regularly through the dry summer while the plant is in growth and fruit. Raised or mounded beds help where drainage is poor.

NSW.intro: Growing dragon fruit in New South Wales. The subtropical far-north coast is the state's dragon fruit country; further south and inland, cold becomes the limit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Dragon fruit wants warm, frost-free conditions, which NSW has on its subtropical northern coast. The frost-sensitive, water-filled stems are the catch: away from the mild coast, on the cold ranges and inland, winter frosts damage or kill unprotected plants. Hot, dry inland districts can grow it with irrigation, but young plants there need some shade so the stems do not scald.

sections.heading: Where dragon fruit grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers, the warm coastal belt around the Tweed, Byron, Lismore and Ballina, is where NSW dragon fruit is concentrated, and there are commercial growers of red-fleshed fruit in the district. The mild coast continues south to around Coffs Harbour on the mid-north coast. Around Sydney and the central coast it becomes a backyard plant for a hot, sheltered corner rather than a paddock crop.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the subtropical north coast the fruit ripens through summer and into autumn, roughly January to May, a little later and shorter than the tropical north because the season is cooler. Plants fruit in flushes, so the picking is spread out. As elsewhere, steady water while the fruit fills stops it splitting.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native right down the NSW coast and well inland, so net or bag fruit and follow local controls. Birds, possums and flying foxes take ripe fruit. The humid coastal summers can bring the same stem and fruit rots seen further north, so keep plants open, airy and off wet ground.

faqs.q: Which dragon fruit varieties suit northern NSW?
faqs.a: On the warm north coast you can grow most types. White-fleshed, self-fertile selections are the most foolproof, and the red-fleshed kinds the district's growers favour for their richer flavour crop well if you plant two varieties and help them along with hand pollination. Whatever you grow, give it a sheltered, frost-free spot.

faqs.q: Can I grow dragon fruit in Sydney or on the cooler NSW coast?
faqs.a: Yes, as a backyard plant in the warmest, most sheltered corner you have, ideally against a sun-facing wall that holds the day's heat. It will be slower and a little later than up north, and in a cold snap a young plant needs cover. A large pot you can shift makes it easier in marginal spots.

VIC.intro: Growing dragon fruit in Victoria. Victoria is not a commercial dragon fruit state; here it is a pot or glasshouse plant for a keen gardener, grown as much for its spectacular flowers as for a dependable crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria sits outside the warm zone dragon fruit needs. Australia's crop is grown in Queensland, the Northern Territory, northern New South Wales and parts of Western Australia, not the cool temperate south. The plant's water-filled stems are frost-sensitive, and Victorian winters, with their frosts and long cool spells, stall growth and damage unprotected plants. Summers are warm enough for growth, but the season is too short and cool for the reliable, repeated fruiting the plant manages in the north.

sections.heading: Where you can grow it in Victoria
sections.body: Your best outdoor chance is the warm, dry inland of the north-west, the Sunraysia district around Mildura and the Murray, where summers are hot, against a sun-facing wall in a frost-free pocket. Across Melbourne and the cooler south, treat dragon fruit as a container plant for a hot courtyard or a heated glasshouse, where you can control the warmth and shelter it from frost.

sections.heading: What to expect in Victoria
sections.body: Even in a good spot, expect a later, lighter and less certain crop than in the north, and in some years mostly the dramatic night flowers rather than fruit. Grow it in a large pot of free-draining cactus mix on a sturdy stake, keep it warm and barely watered over winter, then feed and water through the warm months. Moving the pot under cover or into a glasshouse for winter is the single biggest help in the south.

faqs.q: Why does my dragon fruit flower in Victoria but never fruit?
faqs.a: Two things work against fruit set in the south. The flowers open for just a night and rely on moths, which are scarce on a cold southern night, and many showy cultivars cannot set fruit with their own pollen anyway. Hand pollinate on the evening a flower opens, brushing pollen between two different plants, and you will turn those blooms into fruit far more often.

faqs.q: Is dragon fruit worth growing in Victoria just for the flowers?
faqs.a: Many southern gardeners think so. The huge, perfumed, white night flowers (the plant is also called Queen of the Night) are a spectacle in late summer, opening at dusk and gone by morning. If you get fruit as well, treat it as a bonus, and hand pollinate to improve the odds. It makes a striking, low-water pot plant for a hot courtyard even in a year it does not crop.
